skill.name: nano-banana-edit
skill.description: Edit images with Google Nano Banana 2 (image-to-image edit endpoint) on RunComfy. Documents Nano Banana Edit's strengths (preserve subject identity, swap background, localize edits with spatial language, multi-image batch edits up to 20 inputs), the schema, and when to route to GPT Image 2 edit / Flux Kontext / Nano Banana 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "nano banana edit", "edit with nano banana", "image edit nano banana", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images with Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy. Identity preservation, background swaps, spatial edits, and batch processing up to 20 images per call.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: The SKILL.md file is a legitimate documentation file for using the RunComfy CLI to interact with Google's Nano Banana 2 image editing API. All static analysis findings are false positives: detected 'shell backtick execution' patterns are markdown code blocks containing bash examples, 'hardcoded URLs' are documentation links to runcomfy.com and github.com, 'path traversal sequences' are placeholder text in examples, and 'hidden file in home directory' refers to the documented best practice of storing API tokens in ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600. The file includes explicit security and privacy guidance. No malicious intent or actual executable code is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation references external CLI commands
low_findings.description: The skill documents bash commands for installing and using the runcomfy CLI (npm i -g @runcomfy/cli, runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit). These are instructional examples in markdown code blocks, not executable code. Users must explicitly install and invoke the CLI themselves. No command injection risk as the CLI transmits JSON bodies directly to the API without shell expansion.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear documentation of CLI usage patterns with explicit security note that the CLI does NOT shell-expand prompts, transmitted as JSON to the API.

low_findings.title: Documentation references external URLs
low_findings.description: The file contains URLs to runcomfy.com, github.com, and docs.runcomfy.com for documentation and the model API endpoint (model-api.runcomfy.net). These are legitimate service endpoints documented in the skill's security section with an explicit outbound endpoint whitelist.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs are to the documented runcomfy service and its documentation. The skill explicitly states no telemetry or callbacks are made.

low_findings.title: Token storage in user config directory
low_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores the API token at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600 (owner-only). This is a security best practice, not a risk. An alternative RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable is provided for CI/container use cases to avoid file storage entirely.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Explicit security guidance with proper file permissions (0600) and an env var alternative. This is documented best practice for credential storage.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Image editing workflows require precise identity preservation and batch processing capabilities. This skill provides expert guidance for using Google Nano Banana 2's edit endpoint to swap backgrounds, localize changes, and process up to 20 images per call.
actual_capabilities: Invoke the google/nano-banana-2/edit endpoint via the runcomfy CLI
Preserve subject identity, pose, and clothing while modifying backgrounds
Process batch edits with 1–20 input images per single API call
Localize edits using spatial language like 'left object only' or 'upper-right corner'
Generate 1–4 output images per call with configurable resolution (0.5K to 4K)
Route requests to the correct model (GPT Image 2 edit, Flux Kontext, or Nano Banana 2 t2i) based on task requirements
limitations: Limited to 1–20 input images per API call; the first image is treated as primary
Long compound prompts cause drift; complex edits require multiple passes
Multilingual in-image text edits work better with GPT Image 2 edit
Web search grounding adds latency and cost; enable only when needed

use_cases.title: E-commerce SKU gallery generation
use_cases.description: Generate consistent product images on different backgrounds for online store catalogs. Process 20 SKUs in a single batch with locked framing and resolution.

use_cases.title: Influencer content background swaps
use_cases.description: Replace backgrounds in influencer photos while preserving facial identity, pose, and clothing. Ideal for seasonal campaigns and location changes.
use_cases.target_user: Social media managers and content creators

use_cases.title: A/B testing ad creative variants
use_cases.description: Generate multiple ad creative variants from a single source image using seed locks and the number_of_images parameter for systematic A/B testing.

prompt_templates.title: Background swap with identity preservation
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the subject identity, pose, and clothing unchanged. Convert the background into [target environment description].

prompt_templates.title: Localized object removal
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the [specific object with spatial reference] only. Keep [other elements] unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Targeted object removal using spatial language

prompt_templates.title: Batch product consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: For each input image: keep the product's [key attributes] unchanged. Apply [consistent change] across all images. Center the product at the same fraction of frame as the input.

prompt_templates.title: Text replacement in image
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the [product/scene] and lighting exactly as in the input. Replace only the [text element] from "[old text]" to "[new text]", same font weight, centered, [color] on [background].
prompt_templates.scenario: In-image text editing while preserving layout

output_examples.input: User asks: 'Remove the leftmost object from this still life photo and keep everything else'
output_examples.output: The skill invokes the runcomfy CLI with a prompt specifying spatial language ('Remove the leftmost object only') and preservation goals ('Keep the right two objects, the table, and the lighting unchanged'). The CLI submits the JSON body to the Nano Banana 2 edit endpoint and downloads the result to the specified output directory.

output_examples.input: User asks: 'Generate 5 product variants on different backgrounds for my store'
output_examples.output: The skill routes to the Nano Banana Edit model because batch consistency with identity preservation is the priority. It constructs a batch call with 5 input URLs, locked aspect ratio, and a prompt that specifies 'keep the product identity unchanged' followed by a consistent background change.

best_practices: Lead prompts with preservation goals ('Keep identity/pose/clothing unchanged') before stating the change. Models honor up-front instructions better than tail-end ones.
Use concrete spatial language like 'background only', 'left object', or 'upper-right corner' to localize edits precisely.
Lock aspect_ratio and resolution parameters when editing a batch to ensure consistent output framing across all images.
anti_patterns: Writing long compound instructions like 'change A and B and C and D' in a single prompt causes drift. Split complex edits into multiple passes.
Using passive voice in edit instructions (e.g., 'the background should be changed') produces weaker results than imperative phrasing.
Omitting preservation goals allows the model to subtly rewrite faces, brands, or framing. Always state what should stay the same.

faq.question: What is the difference between Nano Banana Edit and Nano Banana 2 t2i?
faq.answer: Nano Banana Edit is the image-to-image edit endpoint that takes existing images and modifies them. Nano Banana 2 t2i generates new images from text prompts. Use Edit when you have source images; use t2i for generation from scratch.

faq.question: How many images can I process in a single call?
faq.answer: The edit endpoint accepts 1 to 20 input images per call. The first image is treated as the primary reference, and the remaining images provide auxiliary cues for batch consistency.

faq.question: Is my API token stored securely?
faq.answer: Yes. The runcomfy CLI stores the API token at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with file mode 0600 (owner-only read/write). For CI or container environments, set the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable to avoid file storage entirely.

faq.question: Can the model edit multilingual text in images?
faq.answer: Nano Banana Edit handles text replacement but GPT Image 2 edit performs better for multilingual in-image text edits like signs and labels with non-Latin scripts.

faq.question: What should I do if an edit drifts from my intent?
faq.answer: Split the edit into multiple passes. For example, do a background-only change first, then a subject outfit swap in a second pass. This produces cleaner results with the same total cost.

faq.question: What resolution and format options are available?
faq.answer: Output resolution can be 0.5K, 1K, 2K, or 4K. Output formats include PNG, JPEG, and WebP. Aspect ratio can be 'auto' (follows input) or a fixed ratio for batch consistency.
